story:
Was it really him, or was my memory playing tricks? I didn’t want it to be him—too embarrassing—but there I was on a long-ago afternoon in a big-name department store (the kind that’s now a clinic or something), wandering the drugstore aisles alone. The kids must have been in school. I can’t remember if I was looking at shampoo or towels—some ordinary thing—but I do remember the timing: a rough stretch after my husband had walked out for a much younger woman. Sixteen years of forever, gone. I used to joke my best diet was losing 180 pounds of unfaithful husband. Truth is, I’d dropped the extra twenty he always said I should, not because I tried harder, but because I could barely eat. We owned a fitness center, so I’d always worked out; the difference was the layer of sadness shaved off. I looked good without even trying—jeans that finally fit, a light pink Henley, hair probably down. Not a frumpy housewife, not that day.

I glanced up, and across the middle aisle I saw him. Or thought I did. Eighteen, maybe twenty years since high school, and he had a little boy with him. My heart did a flip and the Way-Back Machine kicked in—back to high school, back to fastback cars, back even farther to eighth grade. That was when he first sat beside me on the bus. He was the last stop, I was the first, but after that day he always slid into the seat next to me when he rode. Then I moved ten miles away—an impossible distance at that age. Still, he’d walk into town and call me from the pay phone. Later, when he got his license, he found my doorstep. We dated two years, until he asked for his ring back. By senior prom he circled back, asked me to go, but I was seeing the man I thought would be steady and safe. I told my first love no.

I still caught pieces of his life in the small-town paper—an engagement, a promotion, a birth notice, an address. Not stalking, just how small towns work. We’d only crossed paths once since, me juggling three kids and pregnant with a fourth.

Back in that store, I turned a corner and there he was for real. He knew me, too.

“Oh, hello, Joe. Imagine running into you. It’s been a long time. Is this your little boy? He looks a lot like you.”

“Hi, Mary. It has been a long time. How have you been? You haven’t changed a bit. You’re looking good. This is my nephew—my brother Pat’s boy. He only looks like me.”

“I’ve been fine. Thanks. I have four of my own. They’re growing up so fast.”

“Have four myself. I know what you mean. I’d love to stay and talk, but I’ve got to get Junior to afternoon kindergarten. Maybe we’ll run into each other again.”

“Sure. Bye.”

A brush-off? Maybe. Or maybe the truth. What else was there to say? I couldn’t exactly confess old feelings to a married man. Besides, wasn’t it strange? I’d picked a husband because I believed he wouldn’t leave—and he did. Would Joe have left me all over again? Later a classmate mentioned he’d cheated on his wife. So no, that door I didn’t walk through probably led to the same kind of hurt. The Way-Back Machine shut down. I straightened my pink Henley, pushed my cart, and counted my blessings. Then I moved on.
